title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman charges Texas Alliance for Life director Joe Pojman with idolatry for treating Supreme Court rulings as binding as 'the force of gravity,' arguing that both Scripture and the U.S. Constitution actually demand that states ignore Roe v. Wade and abolish abortion outright.
audiences: Pro-life Christian deciding whether to back abolition bills
State legislator weighing a bill that defies Roe
Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
Pastor or theologian wrestling with civil obedience

questions.question: Why does Silberman accuse Joe Pojman and Texas Alliance for Life of idolatry rather than mere strategic disagreement?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian wrestling with civil obedience
questions.answer: Because Pojman framed obedience to the Supreme Court as a fixed law of nature on par with gravity, which elevates the Court above God. Silberman reads this as making the Court the functional deity to which Pojman owes ultimate allegiance, with God demoted to a temporary ally only when His word agrees with SCOTUS.
questions.quote: In equating obedience to the Supreme Court with a law of nature, Pojman is telling us who his god is. His allegiance to the true and living God is reduced to a temporary alliance whenever God's word happens to match the Supreme Court's opinion.

questions.question: What pattern does Silberman see across pro-life leadership in multiple states?
questions.answer: He says no major pro-life group has backed abolition bills in Oklahoma, Indiana, Idaho, or Texas. Instead they keep returning to the Supreme Court to ask permission to do what is right, treating the Court as the legitimate arbiter of whether murder may be outlawed.
questions.quote: It's why not one prominent pro-life organization has supported the efforts to abolish abortion in Oklahoma, Indiana, Idaho, and now Texas. They think returning every few years to grovel for the Supreme Court's permission to do what's right is the appropriate way to fight the arbitrary federal legalization of murder.

questions.question: What biblical commands does Silberman say make compliance with Roe impossible for Christians?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian deciding whether to back abolition bills
questions.answer: He cites the duty to rescue those being led to slaughter, to bring justice to the fatherless, and to love one's neighbor as oneself. He insists these commands and submission to Roe are mutually exclusive: a Christian can do one or the other, not both.
questions.quote: The Bible tells us to "rescue those being led to slaughter" (Prv 24:10), "bring justice to the fatherless" (Is 1:15), and love our neighbors as ourselves (Matt 22:39). You can do these things or you can submit to Supreme Court opinions stating that murdering certain humans should be legal, but you cannot do both.

questions.question: On constitutional grounds, why does Silberman say Roe v. Wade has no claim on the states?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative skeptical of judicial supremacy
questions.answer: He argues the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause already covers preborn humans, and Article VI says only the Constitution and laws made pursuant to it are legitimate. Roe is neither a law nor pursuant to the Constitution, a point he reinforces by quoting Stanford Law's John Hart Ely dismissing Roe as not even attempting to be constitutional law.
questions.quote: As former dean of Stanford Law John Hart Ely put it, "[Roe v Wade] is not constitutional law and gives almost no sense of an obligation to try to be."

questions.question: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to address the hypothetical that the Constitution itself protected abortion?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian wrestling with civil obedience
questions.answer: He notes Garrison publicly burned the Constitution in 1854 over its slavery compromises, illustrating that a higher moral law against killing innocents would override even the founding document. Silberman then clarifies that no such conflict exists for abortion, so the Constitution can be enlisted alongside, though not as the foundation of, the abolitionist case.
questions.quote: During a July 4th, 1854 speech to the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society, William Lloyd Garrison burned a copy of the US Constitution, among other documents, because of the pre- 13th and 14th amendment compromises which allowed for slavery.

questions.question: What does Silberman propose as the practical course of action for legislators and citizens?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing a bill that defies Roe
questions.answer: Repent of treating SCOTUS as ultimate, ignore Roe, and pass laws abolishing abortion. The decisive consideration should not be fear of judicial reprisal but fear of divine judgment for continuing to allow the killing.
questions.quote: We need to repent of this idolatry. We need to ignore Roe and abolish abortion. This requires less concern over what men will do if we outlaw murder and more concern about what God will do if we don't.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Directly linked from this article as the positive program Silberman wants legislators to pursue instead of bargaining with SCOTUS.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Cited inline as the Oklahoma case study of pro-life leaders opposing abolition, part of the same pattern Silberman names here.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Cited inline as the Indiana instance of the same pro-life-leadership-blocks-abolition pattern.

related.title: Idaho Pro-Life State Senator Says Ending Abortion 'Destroys the Pro-Life Movement'
related.reason: Cited inline as the Idaho example of pro-life politicians defending the status quo against abolition.

related.title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
related.reason: Follow-up coverage of the same Texas HB896 effort that Texas Alliance for Life is opposing in this article.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Develops the broader thesis that mainstream pro-life groups treat abolitionists as threats, the institutional backdrop to Pojman's stance.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Lays out the categorical distinction Silberman is operating from when he criticizes pro-life leadership for submitting to SCOTUS.
